description: Figure 6 presents thermodynamic phase stability data for a low-density δ-ferrite steel (Fe-0.35C-1.47Mn-4.5Al-1.47Ni-0.26Cr-0.28Mo wt.%). Panel (a) shows the iron-carbon equilibrium phase diagram with temperature (600–1400°C) versus carbon content (0–1.5 wt.%), delineating phase fields including BCC (ferrite), FCC (austenite), BCC+FCC, BCC+carbides, and BCC+FCC+θ, with a vertical dashed line near 0.35 wt.% C indicating the alloy composition. Panel (b) displays the calculated volume fraction of each phase as a function of temperature (550–1150°C), where the black curve represents carbides, red represents BCC, and blue represents FCC. The graphs demonstrate that carbides remain stable below approximately 720–750°C, while FCC austenite emerges above this temperature range as carbides dissolve. These calculations explain the experimental observations that low-temperature rolling (650–750°C) preserves ultra-fine spherical carbides within ferrite/carbide composite lamellae, whereas high-temperature rolling (>850°C) leads to complete carbide dissolution and formation of a duplex δ-ferrite/martensite structure, thereby validating the processing–microstructure relationship governing impact toughness.
